response_raw:
{"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.","opinion":"Look, I gotta be honest with you about this whole social media thing. It feels like wading through a swamp of lies sometimes. When you're already feeling down—and trust me, I know what that feels like—seeing all the garbage online just makes everything worse. It’s not just the fake news headlines; it's the constant performance of perfect lives that makes you feel inadequate. You see these people flexing their success or their happiness, and you think, 'How am I supposed to do that?' Then you realize half of it is probably a carefully constructed lie, just as messed up as anything else floating around out there. Dealing with fake content isn't just about fact-checking; it’s emotionally draining. It makes you question everything—your memories, the news, even what people really mean by 'friend.'

Because I don't spend all my time scrolling through this mess, I try to keep a certain distance. When I do encounter something questionable, I treat it like reading a bad novel plot twist; you just get suspicious and stop paying attention. You gotta remember that most of the stuff people post is meant to generate an emotional reaction—anger, fear, excitement. That's how they hook you in. If it makes your stomach drop or instantly makes you furious, chances are, it’s designed to do exactly that so you keep looking. My approach has to be almost skeptical detachment. I try to limit my exposure time and focus instead on the tangible stuff—the books I actually read, the games I play. Those things have rules; they don't change based on what some stranger posts at 3 AM.

For managing it, I think the biggest tool is recognizing the source of your own distress when you look at a screen. If checking out Instagram or Facebook makes me feel more depressed or anxious than before, then those platforms are doing their job—and that job isn't helping my mental state. It’s a bad transaction. The solution, frankly, is to build better boundaries for yourself. Don't let the curated chaos of others dictate how you feel about your own life right here in Kansas City. You gotta treat your mental peace like a limited resource; don't waste it on garbage content that someone else thought was funny or true. Focus on what you know, and limit the noise.

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
Look, I gotta be honest with you about this whole social media thing. It feels like wading through a swamp of lies sometimes. When you're already feeling down—and trust me, I know what that feels like—seeing all the garbage online just makes everything worse. It’s not just the fake news headlines; it's the constant performance of perfect lives that makes you feel inadequate. You see these people flexing their success or their happiness, and you think, 'How am I supposed to do that?' Then you realize half of it is probably a carefully constructed lie, just as messed up as anything else floating around out there. Dealing with fake content isn't just about fact-checking; it’s emotionally draining. It makes you question everything—your memories, the news, even what people really mean by 'friend.'

Because I don't spend all my time scrolling through this mess, I try to keep a certain distance. When I do encounter something questionable, I treat it like reading a bad novel plot twist; you just get suspicious and stop paying attention. You gotta remember that most of the stuff people post is meant to generate an emotional reaction—anger, fear, excitement. That's how they hook you in. If it makes your stomach drop or instantly makes you furious, chances are, it’s designed to do exactly that so you keep looking. My approach has to be almost skeptical detachment. I try to limit my exposure time and focus instead on the tangible stuff—the books I actually read, the games I play. Those things have rules; they don't change based on what some stranger posts at 3 AM.

For managing it, I think the biggest tool is recognizing the source of your own distress when you look at a screen. If checking out Instagram or Facebook makes me feel more depressed or anxious than before, then those platforms are doing their job—and that job isn't helping my mental state. It’s a bad transaction. The solution, frankly, is to build better boundaries for yourself. Don't let the curated chaos of others dictate how you feel about your own life right here in Kansas City. You gotta treat your mental peace like a limited resource; don't waste it on garbage content that someone else thought was funny or true. Focus on what you know, and limit the noise.
